summary: Cathy O'Neil offers a humorous yet philosophical guide to surviving excessive work travel. She argues that business class and airport lounges, while relatively better than coach, are still far worse than being home—a perspective that should temper class resentment between travelers. Beyond practical advice (pack peanut butter crackers, bring less stuff), she explores the psychological toll of constant travel: the shallow interactions, existential angst, and righteous anger that accumulate. Her core prescription is to remain 'open and connected' with strangers, treating each interaction as potentially meaningful, while also working on one's own sense of worthiness.
key_points: Business class should be compared to being home, not to coach—it's still worse than not traveling at all
Airport lounges deliberately prevent you from taking portable snacks, making peanut butter crackers essential packing
Constant travel creates 'righteous anger' and existential angst through endless shallow interactions
The key to surviving overtravel is remaining 'open and connected'—assuming each person you meet might expose something unexpectedly meaningful
Travel's hidden gift is forcing you to confront the hardest existential work: maintaining your own sense of worthiness
money_quotes: Long story short, I travel way. too. fucking. much.
If I wanted to hide an alcoholism problem, then business traveling would be The. Way. To. Do. It.
You'll never regret having less to carry. Unless you forget peanut butter crackers.
Traveling means having multiple shallow interactions with multiple people on a daily basis. It's enough to make you think there's no such thing as love, or meaningful connection.

Assume that the person you're about to meet is interesting, deep, thoughtful, and is about to expose something unexpectedly important and meaningful to you. But also, don't hold it against them at all if they don't.
Being nice is an invitation to conversation as well as a signal that you won't judge, which is even more important.
Interactions between humans, even when they're meaningful and kind, cannot heal one's wounds, and that it's only a person's own sense of worthiness that can ever do that.
Which is really all you can ask about something like travel, that it lets you work on the hardest thing of all, because honestly who has time in their normal lives to work on the hard stuff?
key_insight: The real challenge of constant travel isn't logistics but maintaining genuine human connection and personal worthiness amid endless shallow interactions and displacement from home.
